title: IVIG & SCIG Therapy: A Systematic Approach
objectives: Differentiate indications for IVIG vs SCIG
Recognize adverse effects and premedication strategies
Understand immunoglobulin replacement in primary immunodeficiency

sections.title: IVIG Basics
sections.content:
**Intravenous immunoglobulin — pooled IgG from thousands of donors**

• Replacement dose: 400-600 mg/kg every 3-4 weeks
• Immunomodulatory dose: 1-2 g/kg (higher, for autoimmune conditions)

**Replacement indications:**
• Primary immunodeficiency (CVID, XLA, HIGM)
• Secondary hypogammaglobulinemia (CLL, post-transplant)

**Immunomodulatory indications:**
• ITP, Kawasaki disease, GBS, CIDP, myasthenia gravis
• Mechanism: Fc receptor blockade, anti-idiotype antibodies, complement modulation

sections.title: SCIG Administration
sections.content:
**Subcutaneous immunoglobulin — same product, different route**

• Weekly or biweekly infusions at home
• Dose: Convert from IVIG monthly dose (divide by frequency)
• Steady-state IgG levels (no peaks/troughs like IVIG)
• Lower rate of systemic adverse effects
• Empowers patient independence

**Advantages over IVIG:**
• No IV access needed
• Fewer systemic reactions
• Stable IgG levels
• Home-based

**Disadvantages:**
• More frequent administration
• Local site reactions (swelling, redness)
• Not suitable for high-dose immunomodulatory use

sections.title: Adverse Effects
sections.content:
**IVIG adverse effects:**
• Infusion reactions: Headache, chills, myalgias, nausea (rate-related)
• Aseptic meningitis (high-dose)
• Thromboembolic events (especially with high dose, elderly)
• Renal failure (sucrose-containing products)
• Hemolytic anemia (anti-A/anti-B in non-O blood types)
• Anaphylaxis in IgA-deficient patients with anti-IgA antibodies

**Premedication:**
• Acetaminophen + diphenhydramine
• Slow initial infusion rate
• Hydration pre/post infusion
• IgA-depleted products for IgA-deficient patients

sections.title: Monitoring & Goals
sections.content:
**IgG trough levels:**
• Check before next infusion (trough)
• Target: >500 mg/dL minimum; many aim for >700-800 mg/dL
• Individualize based on infection frequency
• Higher troughs correlate with fewer infections

**Monitoring schedule:**
• IgG trough every 3-6 months initially
• Annual: CBC, CMP, hepatitis serologies
• Assess infection frequency and quality of life

mcqs.stem: A patient undergoes skin prick testing to peanut. The wheal is 8mm with a negative control of 0mm and histamine control of 5mm. How should this result be interpreted?

options.text: Positive test — wheal ≥3mm greater than negative control indicates IgE sensitization
options.explanation: Per AAAAI/ACAAI practice parameters, a wheal ≥3mm greater than the negative control is considered positive. This indicates IgE sensitization but must be correlated with clinical history to diagnose true allergy.

options.text: Negative test — wheal must exceed histamine control to be positive
options.explanation: The positive threshold is compared to the NEGATIVE control (≥3mm difference), not the histamine control. The histamine control validates that the patient can mount a response.

options.text: Equivocal — requires serum specific IgE for confirmation
options.explanation: An 8mm wheal with 0mm negative control is clearly positive (8mm difference). Serum sIgE may complement but is not required for this interpretation.

options.text: Invalid test — the histamine control is too small
options.explanation: A histamine wheal of ≥3mm validates adequate skin reactivity. A 5mm histamine response is normal and validates the test.
